Event: Pierce routinely mispronounces Abed's name as "Ay-bed".
Explanation: He gains nothing tangible while mildly disrespecting and annoying Abed.

Event: Pierce acts like the guy who does or says what everyone else wants but isn’t willing to.
Explanation: He vents and feels bold at others’ social expense.

Event: Pierce misguesses another man's berserk button but reveals his own (hair loss).
Explanation: He exposes his own insecurity without achieving his intended insight about the other person.

Event: Pierce hires LeVar Burton to meet Troy as revenge, knowing it will traumatize Troy.
Explanation: He gets petty revenge and satisfaction while severely distressing Troy.

Event: Pierce posthumously hires Burton to support Troy’s journey when he leaves Greendale.
Explanation: He uses his resources after death to meaningfully help Troy grow.

Event: Pierce lashes out and increasingly antagonizes the group and other students due to resentment at being the Butt-Monkey.
Explanation: His escalating cruelty socially isolates him further while harming everyone else.

Event: Pierce tries to hit on Shirley.
Explanation: He embarrasses himself and makes Shirley uncomfortable without real gain.

Event: Pierce admits Jeff has reasons to hate him and decides to be the group’s bad guy for Jeff’s sake.
Explanation: He accepts blame and consciously absorbs negativity to stabilize Jeff and the group.

Event: Pierce objects to "gay" as a derogatory term after Alan says it to Jeff.
Explanation: He momentarily uses his status to challenge a harmful insult, helping others with little cost.

Event: Pierce helps Britta in "Herstory of Dance" out of shared determination.
Explanation: He invests effort to support Britta’s perseverance, benefiting both.

Event: Pierce openly and hopelessly lusts after the much younger women in the group.
Explanation: His behavior creeps them out and damages his image without yielding real benefits.

Event: Pierce theorizes Shirley may be pregnant with Chang's baby just because she voted against him.
Explanation: He creates gratuitous distress and conflict while only feeding his paranoia and resentment.

Event: Pierce runs for student body president solely to publicly humiliate Vicki for not lending him a pencil.
Explanation: He gets a platform for petty revenge at great humiliation to Vicki.

Event: Pierce fakes his own death and torments each study-group member with psychologically targeted "gifts".
Explanation: His elaborate scheme traumatises friends and further alienates him, offering no lasting benefit.

Event: Pierce tries to strip the last remaining joy from suicidal Neil and bullies him over his weight in AD&D.
Explanation: He sadistically attacks a vulnerable person, deepening his own ostracism and risking Neil’s life.

Event: Pierce flees to Canada to avoid the Vietnam War draft.
Explanation: He preserves his own safety while arguably shifting burdens onto others and earning social stigma.

Event: Pierce tells Jeff he’s being a bad friend by overusing “Britta” as an insult.
Explanation: He correctly calls out destructive behavior, helping Britta and nudging Jeff with minimal cost.

Event: Pierce repeatedly complains to his mother about Jeff bullying him and lies that he has a girlfriend in Canada.
Explanation: He gains emotional support and spares her some worry via a mostly harmless lie.

Event: Pierce rejects his father’s inheritance so Gilbert can have it, recognizing the racist abuse Gilbert suffered.
Explanation: He sacrifices material gain to repair injustice and give Gilbert resources and validation.

Event: Pierce expresses horror when Leonard’s gang abandons a senile old man to take the fall.
Explanation: His moral objection costs him nothing and affirms concern for a vulnerable person.

Event: Pierce is disgusted by Duncan’s bullying of catatonic Abed.
Explanation: He recognizes and opposes excessive cruelty, emotionally siding with Abed.

Event: Pierce thinks Jeff’s implication that all black people are in prison goes too far.
Explanation: He identifies an unacceptable boundary, slightly improving norms.

Event: Pierce encourages Shirley to overcome her fear of public speaking with sales-pitch exercises.
Explanation: He uses his skills to coach her, boosting both his usefulness and her confidence.

Event: Pierce tries to force the term "streets ahead" to become hip.
Explanation: His push makes him look out-of-touch and annoys others without real benefit.

Event: Pierce talks about Jeff bullying him to his mother and seeks her sympathy.
Explanation: He gains comfort but reinforces his victim mindset without solving the relationship.

Event: Pierce chooses to help the group beat his father in "Digital Estate Planning" and urges Gilbert to stand up too.
Explanation: He allies with them against Cornelius, healing relationships and confronting abuse.

Event: Pierce happily embraces Gilbert as the brother he always wanted.
Explanation: He gains family connection and gives Gilbert acceptance and affection.

Event: Pierce accurately predicts the future of video games and pitches the idea to his father.
Explanation: His foresight could benefit both, though Cornelius’ rejection blunts the outcome.

Event: Pierce tells Shirley that Chang might be the father of her baby, partly out of spite.
Explanation: He delivers necessary but painful information in the most hurtful way, mixing truth with cruelty.

Event: Pierce often talks to his mother about his feelings of being bullied by Jeff.
Explanation: He gets some emotional release but entrenches his grievance without resolving it.

Event: Pierce buys Troy and Abed’s handshake just so he can ruin it.
Explanation: He gets petty satisfaction while damaging their harmless joy and closeness.

Event: Pierce sleeps with the Spanish teacher to help his friends pass the class.
Explanation: He sacrifices some dignity for grades, and the group benefits academically.

Event: Pierce pretends to overdose on pills to play manipulative mind games.
Explanation: His fake crisis traumatizes others and destroys trust while providing only fleeting attention.

Event: Pierce breaks both his legs and gets the trampoline removed after discovering Jeff and Troy's "secret trampoline".
Explanation: He injures himself and ruins a shared joy through meddling.

Event: Pierce goes off-script in an anti-drug PSA and excites middle-schoolers about drugs.
Explanation: He undermines the PSA’s goal and damages his reputation without any clear gain.

Event: Pierce crashes the Dungeons & Dragons suicide-intervention game and nearly derails it.
Explanation: He jeopardizes a life-saving plan and deepens his exclusion.

Event: Jeff uses Pierce as a decoy in paintball by telling him not to come over, knowing he will.
Explanation: Jeff gains a tactical advantage at a clear physical cost to Pierce.

Event: Pierce fakes a heart attack twice to win the paintball game.
Explanation: He secures personal victory by deceiving others and exploiting their concern.

Event: Pierce does the group’s history project alone after they abandon him for their own plots.
Explanation: He demonstrates competence and responsibility, helping everyone’s grade and slightly redeeming himself.

Event: Pierce falsely admits to getting Jeff kicked out of Kane’s class so the group can blame him instead of Jeff.
Explanation: He voluntarily becomes a scapegoat to preserve group cohesion and give Jeff an excuse.

Event: Pierce arranges for Sophie B. Hawkins to appear at Britta’s dance to defend her against Jeff’s mockery.
Explanation: He spends effort/resources to vindicate Britta and punish Jeff’s cruelty.

Event: Pierce gives Annie a tiara as a genuine, non-manipulative gift.
Explanation: He expresses sincere affection, strengthening that relationship at no real cost.

Event: Pierce leaves supportive gifts and kind words for Britta, Shirley, Annie, and Troy in his will.
Explanation: His bequests affirm respect and affection, improving their closure and his posthumous image.

Event: Pierce uses a posthumous polygraph test to turn the group against each other.
Explanation: His final scheme causes conflict and pain without benefiting him.

Event: Pierce leaves only an insult and sperm for Abed in his will.
Explanation: He expends effort to demean Abed, worsening Abed’s treatment and his own legacy.

Event: Pierce leaves Jeff a "gift" that’s just one last gay accusation.
Explanation: He forgoes a chance at reconciliation for a cheap jab that yields no benefit.

Event: Pierce fills multiple canisters of sperm for his will, causing himself to die of dehydration.
Explanation: His overcommitment to a bizarre bequest literally kills him, though others gain material/closure.

Event: Pierce helps Shirley plan a sandwich shop in Season 3.
Explanation: He channels his business savvy into a shared dream, strengthening their bond and prospects.
